section.title: Food Sacrificed to Idols
section.sectionRationale: The Corinthians boasted that they knew idols were nothing, so eating food offered to them was harmless. Paul agrees with the theology, there is one God the Father and one Lord Jesus Christ through whom all things exist, but he confronts the arrogance of a knowledge that wounds. Love, not mere knowledge, must govern the use of Christian freedom, for the weak brother whose conscience is defiled is one for whom Christ died. To wound him is to sin against Christ.
section.thesis: Knowledge that there is one God and one Lord Jesus Christ is true, but knowledge alone puffs up while love builds up; so Christian freedom must be ruled by love for the weak brother for whom Christ died, since to wound his conscience is to sin against Christ.
section.frameNote: Three movements: knowledge puffs up but love builds up; the one God and the one Lord over against idols that are nothing; and the weak brother for whom Christ died, whom love will not wound.

units.label: Knowledge and love
units.note: knowledge puffs up, love builds up

verses.text: Now about {{eidolothyton|food sacrificed to idols}}: We know that we all have {{gnosis|knowledge}}. Knowledge puffs up, but love {{oikodomeo|builds up}}.
The one who thinks he knows something does not yet know as he ought to know.
But the one who loves God is known by God.
So about eating food sacrificed to idols: We know that an idol is nothing at all in the world, and that there is no God but one.
For even if there are so-called gods, whether in heaven or on earth (as there are many so-called gods and lords),
yet for us there is but one God, the Father, from whom all things came and for whom we exist. And there is but one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom all things came and through whom we exist.

units.label: The weak brother for whom Christ died

verses.text: But not everyone has this knowledge. Some people are still so accustomed to idols that they eat such food as if it were sacrificed to an idol. And since their {{suneidesis|conscience}} is weak, it is defiled.
But food does not bring us closer to God: We are no worse if we do not eat, and no better if we do.
Be careful, however, that your freedom does not become a {{proskomma|stumbling block}} to the weak.
For if someone with a weak conscience sees you who are well informed eating in an idol’s temple, will he not be encouraged to eat food sacrificed to idols?
So this weak brother, for whom Christ died, is destroyed by your knowledge.
By sinning against your brothers in this way and wounding their weak conscience, you sin against Christ.
Therefore, if what I eat causes my brother to stumble, I will never eat meat again, so that I will not cause him to stumble.

eidolothyton.gloss: Food, especially meat, that has been sacrificed to an idol. Much of the meat in a Greek city passed through pagan temples before reaching the market or the banquet. The question was whether a Christian could eat it, and the answer turns not only on truth but on love.

gnosis.gloss: Knowledge, understanding. The Corinthians prized it, and their knowledge that idols are nothing was correct. But Paul warns that knowledge by itself inflates the ego; only love, joined to knowledge, actually builds up the church.

oikodomeo.gloss: To build up, to edify, literally to construct a house. Set against puffing up, this word pictures love as the true builder of the community. The test of any exercise of freedom is whether it constructs and strengthens others or merely swells oneself.

suneidesis.gloss: Conscience, the inner sense of right and wrong. A weak conscience is one not yet fully persuaded that idols are nothing, so eating such food feels like real participation in idolatry. To press such a person to act against conscience wounds and defiles them.

proskomma.gloss: A stumbling block, an obstacle that causes someone to trip and fall. Paul warns that a believer's freedom, though legitimate, can become the very thing that trips a weaker brother into sin against his conscience, and so must be surrendered out of love.

basic.context: In Corinth most meat had first been offered in pagan temples. Some Christians said, we know idols are nothing, so we can eat whatever we like. Paul agrees that there is only one God and one Lord, so an idol is nothing. But he warns that knowing this can make people proud and careless. A newer believer, not yet free of his old idol worship, might be led by their example to eat against his conscience and be wounded. Since Christ died for that brother, to wound him is to sin against Christ. Love, not just knowledge, must decide how we use our freedom.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul affirms the strong believers' theology but corrects their attitude. The issue is not merely who is right about idols, but whether love governs the use of that rightness. The passage teaches a principle far beyond meat: the strong must limit their liberty for the sake of the weak, because people matter more than the exercise of a freedom.
basic.originalLanguages: The subject is eidolothyton, food sacrificed to idols. The Corinthians boasted in gnosis, knowledge, but Paul says love, not knowledge, does the true oikodomeo, building up. The weak have a tender suneidesis, conscience, not yet sure idols are nothing. And the strong must not let their freedom become a proskomma, a stumbling block, that trips a brother into sin.
basic.typology: The great confession that there is one God the Father takes up Israel's Shema, hear O Israel, the LORD our God, the LORD is one, and astonishingly places the one Lord Jesus Christ within it. The self-giving of the strong for the weak follows the pattern of Christ, who did not please Himself but died for the weak.
basic.christ: Christ is confessed as the one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom all things came and through whom we exist, sharing the identity of the one God. And Christ is the measure of love for the weak: the brother in danger is one for whom Christ died, so to wound him is to sin against Christ Himself. The cross both reveals God and rules our relationships.
basic.god: There is one God, the Father, the source and goal of all things. The so-called gods of the nations are nothing before Him. This one God is not served by proud knowledge that harms others but by love that builds up, for the one who loves God is known by God.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 6 gives the Shema that Paul reshapes around Christ. Romans 14 is Paul's parallel teaching on the weak and the strong. 1 Corinthians 10 returns to food and idols, warning against the table of demons. Philippians 2 shows Christ not pleasing Himself, the pattern of surrendered freedom.
basic.oneStory: The one God who called Israel to confess Him alone is now known as Father and as the one Lord Jesus Christ. In His name the idols are exposed as nothing, and His self-giving love becomes the rule for His people: the strong lay down their rights for the weak, because Christ laid down His life for them all.

context.literary: This begins a long section, chapters 8 to 10, on food offered to idols and the limits of Christian freedom, opening under the now about heading that answers the Corinthians' letter. Chapter 8 sets the principle, knowledge versus love; chapter 9 offers Paul's own surrender of rights as an example; chapter 10 returns with warnings about idolatry and the Lord's table. The unit moves from a slogan about knowledge to the confession of one God and one Lord, and then to the weak brother.
context.historical: In a Greco-Roman city, temple sacrifice supplied the meat markets and shaped social banquets held in temple precincts. To refuse such food could mean social and economic cost; to eat it freely could confuse new converts recently delivered from idol worship. Paul navigates a real and daily pressure with theological clarity and pastoral care.
context.canonical: Paul's teaching parallels Romans 14 to 15 on the weak and the strong and connects to the apostolic decree of Acts 15 regarding food offered to idols. The confession of verse 6 is one of the New Testament's clearest inclusions of Jesus within the identity of the one God of the Shema, a cornerstone of Christian monotheism.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To affirm the truth that idols are nothing and there is one God and one Lord, while correcting a knowledge that harms, and to establish that love for the weak brother, for whom Christ died, must govern the use of Christian freedom.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The slogans about knowledge are quoted and qualified, not simply endorsed. The abiding prescriptions are that love, not knowledge, must rule; that the strong must not wound the weak; and that surrendering a genuine freedom for a brother's sake is the way of Christ. Paul's own vow never to eat meat models, not legislates, the principle.

debates.question: If an idol is nothing, is there any spiritual reality behind pagan sacrifice?

views.label: Idols are nothing, mere lifeless images
views.case: Verse 4 says an idol is nothing at all in the world, since there is only one God; the images have no divine reality.

views.label: Demonic deception stands behind idolatry
views.case: In 10:20 Paul says pagan sacrifices are offered to demons; the idol is not a god, yet a real spiritual danger lurks behind false worship.

debates.bearing: Both are Paul's own teaching held together: idols are not gods and the food is not magically tainted, yet idolatry is spiritually perilous, so freedom must be exercised with both truth and caution.

debates.question: How far must the strong limit their freedom for the weak?

views.label: Love voluntarily surrenders a real right
views.case: Paul vows never to eat meat again rather than trip a brother, showing love willingly gives up legitimate liberty for another's good.

views.label: Freedom remains, but love governs its use
views.case: The right to eat is not denied; it is subordinated to love, exercised or set aside according to what builds up the weaker believer.

debates.bearing: Either way the principle stands: a genuine freedom must never be wielded in a way that destroys someone for whom Christ died; love outranks the assertion of rights.

study.originalLanguages: The question concerns eidolothyton, food sacrificed to idols, and the Corinthians answered it with gnosis, knowledge, boasting that idols are nothing. Paul grants the truth but warns that knowledge inflates while love does the real oikodomeo, building up. The weaker believer has a tender suneidesis, a conscience not yet freed from idol associations, which is defiled if pressed to eat. So the strong must guard against letting their freedom become a proskomma, a stumbling block that trips a brother for whom Christ died. Truth about the one God and one Lord must be carried by love.

typology.type: The Shema, the one God of Israel
typology.fulfillment: Taken up in verse 6 and expanded to confess the one Lord Jesus Christ within the identity of the one God, the heart of Christian monotheism.
Follows the pattern of Christ, who did not please Himself but died for the weak, so His people surrender their rights for one another.

study.christ: Verse 6 places Jesus at the center of Israel's confession of the one God: there is one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom all things came and through whom we exist. He is the agent of creation and of our new existence, sharing the identity of the one God the Father. And the whole appeal to love is anchored in the cross: the weak brother is one for whom Christ died, so wounding him is sinning against Christ. Christ is both the object of our confession and the measure of our love.
study.god: There is one God, the Father, from whom all things came and for whom we exist, the source and goal of everything. The many so-called gods are nothing before Him. This God is not honored by a proud knowledge that harms the weak, but by love, for the one who loves God is known by God. True theology bears fruit in humble, upbuilding love.

crossReferences.note: Hear O Israel, the LORD our God, the LORD is one, the Shema Paul reshapes around the one God and one Lord in verse 6.
Paul's parallel teaching not to put a stumbling block before a brother, nor to destroy by food one for whom Christ died.
Idols are nothing, yet pagan sacrifices are offered to demons, holding truth and danger together.
Christ did not please Himself but emptied Himself, the pattern of surrendering rights for others' good.
Knowledge without love is nothing; love builds up, the principle at the heart of this chapter.

study.oneStory: The God who called Israel to confess that the LORD is one is now revealed as the Father and as the one Lord Jesus Christ, through whom all things exist. Before Him the idols of the nations are exposed as nothing. Yet the mark of belonging to this God is not proud knowledge but love, patterned on the Christ who died even for the weak. So the strong lay down their freedom for a brother, and the confession of one God and one Lord becomes visible in self-giving love.

quiz.q: What does Paul say is the difference between knowledge and love?

options.text: Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up.
options.note: Verse 1 contrasts knowledge that inflates with love that edifies.

options.text: Knowledge is worthless and love is all that matters.
options.note: Paul affirms the truth they know; he insists love must accompany it, not replace it.

options.text: Love puffs up and knowledge builds up.
options.note: This reverses Paul's words.

options.text: Both knowledge and love always build up equally.
options.note: Only love is said to build up; knowledge alone can inflate.

quiz.q: What does Paul confess about God and Christ in verse 6?

options.text: There is one God the Father and one Lord Jesus Christ, through whom all things exist.
options.note: Verse 6 places Jesus within the identity of the one God of Israel.

options.text: There are many gods and many lords for Christians.
options.note: Paul says the so-called gods are nothing; for us there is one God and one Lord.

options.text: Jesus is one lord among several equal powers.
options.note: Paul confesses Jesus as the one Lord through whom all things came.

options.text: The Father alone is God and Christ is merely a creature.
options.note: Paul joins the one Lord Jesus to the one God, sharing the work of creation.

quiz.q: Why should the strong believer limit his freedom to eat?

options.text: So his freedom does not become a stumbling block that wounds the weak.
options.note: Verse 9 warns that freedom can trip a weaker brother into sin.

options.text: Because eating such food is always sinful.
options.note: Paul says food itself does not bring us closer or further from God.

options.text: Because knowledge does not matter at all.
options.note: Knowledge matters, but it must be governed by love for the weak.

options.text: Because the strong believer will lose his salvation by eating.
options.note: The danger is to the weak brother's conscience, not the strong one's salvation.

quiz.q: How serious is it to wound a weak brother's conscience?

options.text: It is a sin against Christ, for the brother is one for whom Christ died.
options.note: Verses 11 to 12 say wounding the weak brother is sinning against Christ.

options.text: It is a minor matter of personal preference.
options.note: Paul treats it with utmost seriousness as sin against Christ.

options.text: It concerns only the weak brother, not Christ.
options.note: Paul says it is ultimately sin against Christ Himself.

options.text: It matters only if the brother actually falls away.
options.note: The wounding of the conscience itself is already sin against Christ.

quiz.q: What does Paul say he would do rather than cause a brother to stumble?

options.text: He would never eat meat again, surrendering his freedom out of love.
options.note: Verse 13 shows Paul willing to give up a real right for a brother's sake.

options.text: He would insist on his rights regardless of the weak.
options.note: Paul does the opposite, laying down his freedom.

options.text: He would demand the weak brother grow stronger first.
options.note: Paul places the burden on the strong to yield, not on the weak to catch up.

options.text: He would eat privately but not publicly.
options.note: His vow is more sweeping: never eat meat if it trips his brother.

questions: Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up. Where might you be more concerned with being right than with building others up?
Paul confesses one God the Father and one Lord Jesus Christ. How does placing Jesus at the center of your view of God shape your worship and daily life?
A weak brother's conscience can be wounded by another's freedom. Whose conscience should you be more careful of, and how might your example be affecting them?
Paul was willing to give up meat entirely for a brother's sake. What genuine freedom might love call you to lay down for someone weaker in the faith?
To wound a brother for whom Christ died is to sin against Christ. How does remembering that Christ died for the person in front of you change the way you treat them?

translationNotes.note: Food sacrificed to idols renders a single term for meat that had passed through pagan sacrifice; the phrase names the practical question behind chapters 8 to 10.
One God, the Father, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, reshapes the Shema of Deuteronomy 6:4, including Jesus within the identity of the one God, a foundational statement of Christian faith.
For whom Christ died grounds the appeal: the weak brother is not expendable but one Christ valued enough to die for, so wounding him is sin against Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 8:1-13; any em dashes rendered as commas per the no-dash style rule, with wording unchanged.
